title: Two Sum (Introduction to Hashing)
description:
## Introduction to Hashing for Look-ups

In many array problems, the naive approach takes O(n^2) time because for every element, we loop through the rest of the array. **Hashing** allows us to reduce this look-up time to O(1) space, trading space for time.

### The 'Two Sum' Problem
Given an array of integers `nums` and an integer `target`, return indices of the two numbers such that they add up to `target`.

You may assume that each input would have exactly one solution, and you may not use the same element twice. You can return the answer in any order.

### Naive Approach: O(n^2)
Use two nested loops. For every `nums[i]`, iterate through `nums[j]` where `j > i` and check if `nums[i] + nums[j] == target`.

### Optimal Approach (GfG Recommended): O(n)
Use a Hash Map (Dictionary in Python). As you iterate over the array, calculate the `complement = target - nums[i]`.

1. If the `complement` is already in the Hash Map, you've found the two numbers! Return their indices.
2. If not, add `nums[i]` to the Hash Map with its index.

This guarantees a single pass through the array.

title: Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock
description: Find maximum profit from buying and selling stock once.

examples.explanation: Buy on day 2 (price = 1) and sell on day 5 (price = 6)

solution:
def maxProfit(prices):
    min_price = float('inf')
    max_profit = 0
    for price in prices:
        min_price = min(min_price, price)
        max_profit = max(max_profit, price - min_price)
    return max_profit

title: Contains Duplicate
description: Return true if any value appears at least twice in the array.
solution:
def containsDuplicate(nums):
    return len(nums) != len(set(nums))

title: Product of Array Except Self
description: Return an array where each element is the product of all elements except itself.
solution:
def productExceptSelf(nums):
    n = len(nums)
    result = [1] * n
    left = 1
    for i in range(n):
        result[i] = left
        left *= nums[i]
    right = 1
    for i in range(n-1, -1, -1):
        result[i] *= right
        right *= nums[i]
    return result

title: Reverse Linked List
description: Reverse a singly linked list.
solution:
def reverseList(head):
    prev = None
    while head:
        next_node = head.next
        head.next = prev
        prev = head
        head = next_node
    return prev

title: Two Sum (Introduction to Hashing) II - Variation 4
description:
This is a variation of the classic Two Sum (Introduction to Hashing) problem, but with different constraints or a larger dataset.

## Introduction to Hashing for Look-ups

In many array problems, the naive approach takes O(n^2) time because for every element, we loop through the rest of the array. **Hashing** allows us to reduce this look-up time to O(1) space, trading space for time.

### The 'Two Sum' Problem
Given an array of integers `nums` and an integer `target`, return indices of the two numbers such that they add up to `target`.

You may assume that each input would have exactly one solution, and you may not use the same element twice. You can return the answer in any order.

### Naive Approach: O(n^2)
Use two nested loops. For every `nums[i]`, iterate through `nums[j]` where `j > i` and check if `nums[i] + nums[j] == target`.

### Optimal Approach (GfG Recommended): O(n)
Use a Hash Map (Dictionary in Python). As you iterate over the array, calculate the `complement = target - nums[i]`.

1. If the `complement` is already in the Hash Map, you've found the two numbers! Return their indices.
2. If not, add `nums[i]` to the Hash Map with its index.

This guarantees a single pass through the array.

title: Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock II - Variation 4
description:
This is a variation of the classic Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock problem, but with different constraints or a larger dataset.

Find maximum profit from buying and selling stock once.

examples.explanation: Buy on day 2 (price = 1) and sell on day 5 (price = 6)

solution:
def maxProfit(prices):
    min_price = float('inf')
    max_profit = 0
    for price in prices:
        min_price = min(min_price, price)
        max_profit = max(max_profit, price - min_price)
    return max_profit

title: Product of Array Except Self II - Variation 4
description:
This is a variation of the classic Product of Array Except Self problem, but with different constraints or a larger dataset.

Return an array where each element is the product of all elements except itself.
solution:
def productExceptSelf(nums):
    n = len(nums)
    result = [1] * n
    left = 1
    for i in range(n):
        result[i] = left
        left *= nums[i]
    right = 1
    for i in range(n-1, -1, -1):
        result[i] *= right
        right *= nums[i]
    return result

title: Two Sum (Introduction to Hashing) II - Variation 7
description:
This is a variation of the classic Two Sum (Introduction to Hashing) problem, but with different constraints or a larger dataset.

## Introduction to Hashing for Look-ups

In many array problems, the naive approach takes O(n^2) time because for every element, we loop through the rest of the array. **Hashing** allows us to reduce this look-up time to O(1) space, trading space for time.

### The 'Two Sum' Problem
Given an array of integers `nums` and an integer `target`, return indices of the two numbers such that they add up to `target`.

You may assume that each input would have exactly one solution, and you may not use the same element twice. You can return the answer in any order.

### Naive Approach: O(n^2)
Use two nested loops. For every `nums[i]`, iterate through `nums[j]` where `j > i` and check if `nums[i] + nums[j] == target`.

### Optimal Approach (GfG Recommended): O(n)
Use a Hash Map (Dictionary in Python). As you iterate over the array, calculate the `complement = target - nums[i]`.

1. If the `complement` is already in the Hash Map, you've found the two numbers! Return their indices.
2. If not, add `nums[i]` to the Hash Map with its index.

This guarantees a single pass through the array.

title: Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock II - Variation 7
description:
This is a variation of the classic Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock problem, but with different constraints or a larger dataset.

Find maximum profit from buying and selling stock once.

examples.explanation: Buy on day 2 (price = 1) and sell on day 5 (price = 6)

solution:
def maxProfit(prices):
    min_price = float('inf')
    max_profit = 0
    for price in prices:
        min_price = min(min_price, price)
        max_profit = max(max_profit, price - min_price)
    return max_profit

## Introduction to Hashing for Look-ups

In many array problems, the naive approach takes O(n^2) time because for every element, we loop through the rest of the array. **Hashing** allows us to reduce this look-up time to O(1) space, trading space for time.

### The 'Two Sum' Problem
Given an array of integers `nums` and an integer `target`, return indices of the two numbers such that they add up to `target`.

You may assume that each input would have exactly one solution, and you may not use the same element twice. You can return the answer in any order.

### Naive Approach: O(n^2)
Use two nested loops. For every `nums[i]`, iterate through `nums[j]` where `j > i` and check if `nums[i] + nums[j] == target`.

### Optimal Approach (GfG Recommended): O(n)
Use a Hash Map (Dictionary in Python). As you iterate over the array, calculate the `complement = target - nums[i]`.

1. If the `complement` is already in the Hash Map, you've found the two numbers! Return their indices.
2. If not, add `nums[i]` to the Hash Map with its index.

This guarantees a single pass through the array.

title: Two Sum (Introduction to Hashing) II - Variation 12
description:
This is a variation of the classic Two Sum (Introduction to Hashing) problem, but with different constraints or a larger dataset.

## Introduction to Hashing for Look-ups

In many array problems, the naive approach takes O(n^2) time because for every element, we loop through the rest of the array. **Hashing** allows us to reduce this look-up time to O(1) space, trading space for time.

### The 'Two Sum' Problem
Given an array of integers `nums` and an integer `target`, return indices of the two numbers such that they add up to `target`.

You may assume that each input would have exactly one solution, and you may not use the same element twice. You can return the answer in any order.

### Naive Approach: O(n^2)
Use two nested loops. For every `nums[i]`, iterate through `nums[j]` where `j > i` and check if `nums[i] + nums[j] == target`.

### Optimal Approach (GfG Recommended): O(n)
Use a Hash Map (Dictionary in Python). As you iterate over the array, calculate the `complement = target - nums[i]`.

1. If the `complement` is already in the Hash Map, you've found the two numbers! Return their indices.
2. If not, add `nums[i]` to the Hash Map with its index.

This guarantees a single pass through the array.

title: Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock II - Variation 12
description:
This is a variation of the classic Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock problem, but with different constraints or a larger dataset.

Find maximum profit from buying and selling stock once.

examples.explanation: Buy on day 2 (price = 1) and sell on day 5 (price = 6)

solution:
def maxProfit(prices):
    min_price = float('inf')
    max_profit = 0
    for price in prices:
        min_price = min(min_price, price)
        max_profit = max(max_profit, price - min_price)
    return max_profit

title: Product of Array Except Self II - Variation 12
description:
This is a variation of the classic Product of Array Except Self problem, but with different constraints or a larger dataset.

Return an array where each element is the product of all elements except itself.
solution:
def productExceptSelf(nums):
    n = len(nums)
    result = [1] * n
    left = 1
    for i in range(n):
        result[i] = left
        left *= nums[i]
    right = 1
    for i in range(n-1, -1, -1):
        result[i] *= right
        right *= nums[i]
    return result

title: Reverse Linked List II - Variation 12
description:
This is a variation of the classic Reverse Linked List problem, but with different constraints or a larger dataset.

Reverse a singly linked list.
solution:
def reverseList(head):
    prev = None
    while head:
        next_node = head.next
        head.next = prev
        prev = head
        head = next_node
    return prev
